coredata.dc:title: Polydopamine-carbon dots functionalized hollow carbon nanoplatform for fluorescence-imaging and photothermal-enhanced thermochemotherapy
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Junya
Wang, Kaili
Lei, Wei
Mao, Yuling
Di, Donghua
Zhao, Qinfu
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: The low power photothermal therapy can reduce the tissue damage caused by laser irradiation, thus the near-infrared (NIR) absorbing vehicles with high photothermal conversion efficiency are demanded in the low power treatment. Herein, the NIR-absorbing agent polydopamine (PDA) and carbon dots (CDs) were gated on the openings of hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) to construct a photothermal enhanced multi-functional system (HMC-SS-PDA@CDs). Interestingly, the fluorescence emission wavelength of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was red-shifted by FRET effect between PDA and CDs, which solved the dilemma of fluorescence quenching of carbon-based materials and was more conducive to cell imaging. The modification of PDA@CDs not only acts as the gatekeepers to realize multi-responsive release of pH, GSH and NIR, but also endows the HMC vehicle with excellent photothermal generation capacity, the possibility for bio-imaging as well as the enhanced stability. Naturally, both the cytological level and the multicellular tumor sphere level demonstrate that the delivery system has good low-power synergistic therapeutic with combination index (CI) of 0.348 and imaging effects. Meanwhile, the combined treatment group showed the highest tumor inhibition rate of 92.6% at 0.75 W/cm2. Therefore, DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nano-platform had broad application prospects in low power therapy and convenient imaging of carbon-based materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots combined polydopamine
Low power photothermal therapy
Fluorescence imaging
Photothermal-enhanced thermochemotherapy
Hollow mesoporous carbon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The multi-stimuli triggered release of DOX
The shedding mechanism of PDA and CDs
In vitro photothermal properties of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs
Shedding mechanism of PDA and CDs
Photothermal therapeutic effect on cytological level

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) AFM image of CDs; (B) the height profile along the line in (A); (C) three-dimensional AFM results of CDs; TEM of (D) CDs, (E) HMC-COOH, (F) HMC-SS-PDA, (G) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs.

(A) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (the inset of pore size distribution curves); (B) Zeta potentials results; (C) FT-IR spectra; Fluorescence emission spectra of (D) CDs and (E) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; (F) Size and PDI results in pH 7.4 PBS; UV–Vis absorption spectrum, excitation and emission spectra of (G) CDs and (H) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; (I) Size distribution results.

Temperature variation curves of gradient concentrations of (A) HMC-COOH and (D) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs at 0.75 W/cm2; Different laser powers of (B) HMC-COOH and (E) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; Photothermal stability of (C) HMC-COOH and (F) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; Linear time data versus –lnθ of (G) HMC-COOH and (H) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (the inserted curves were the temperature variation of vehicles); (I) The photographs and thermal images of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs supernatant at different conditions (A) different pH media and (B) different concentrations of GSH; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the PDA-free supernatant; (D) The diagram of shedding mechanism of PDA@CDs from HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; The temperature curves of the supernatant in (E) different pH conditions, (F) different concentrations of GSH and (G) PDA-free supernatant.

(A) XRD patterns of DOX, DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs, PM, HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; Drug release curves of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs in (B) various media, (C) pH 7.4 PBS under different conditions and (D) pH 5.0 PBS under different conditions.

(A) Images of AM/PI after co-incubation of 4 T1 cells with HMC-SS-PDA@CDs irradiated for different times (0.75 W/cm2); Cellular uptake analysis by CLSM (B) and FCM (C); Cell viability of 4 T1 cells with (D) and without (E) NIR laser irradiation (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001).
(A) Microscopy photographs of 3D MCS undergoing different treatments; (B) AM/PI images of 3D MCS with different conditions; (C) The corresponding quantitative analysis of cell death/viability.

(A) Fluorescence images and (B) mean fluorescence intensities of main organs and tumors after intravenous injection for 2 h, 6 h and 12 h; (C) Tumor growth curves, (D) relative tumor mass and (E) body weight of different groups; (F) Tumor photographs and (G) H&E staining images of major organs and tumor tissue sections after 15 d of treatment (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001).

(A) Preparation procedures of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs drug delivery nanoplatform; (B) Schematic illustration of triple stimuli-responsive release, fluorescence-imaging and photothermal enhanced synergistic thermochemotherapy.
IC50 values of different treatments for 4T1 cells after 24 h incubation.

head.ce:title: Polydopamine-carbon dots functionalized hollow carbon nanoplatform for fluorescence-imaging and photothermal-enhanced thermochemotherapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low power photothermal therapy can reduce the tissue damage caused by laser irradiation, thus the near-infrared (NIR) absorbing vehicles with high photothermal conversion efficiency are demanded in the low power treatment. Herein, the NIR-absorbing agent polydopamine (PDA) and carbon dots (CDs) were gated on the openings of hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) to construct a photothermal enhanced multi-functional system (HMC-SS-PDA@CDs). Interestingly, the fluorescence emission wavelength of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was red-shifted by FRET effect between PDA and CDs, which solved the dilemma of fluorescence quenching of carbon-based materials and was more conducive to cell imaging. The modification of PDA@CDs not only acts as the gatekeepers to realize multi-responsive release of pH, GSH and NIR, but also endows the HMC vehicle with excellent photothermal generation capacity, the possibility for bio-imaging as well as the enhanced stability. Naturally, both the cytological level and the multicellular tumor sphere level demonstrate that the delivery system has good low-power synergistic therapeutic with combination index (CI) of 0.348 and imaging effects. Meanwhile, the combined treatment group showed the highest tumor inhibition rate of 92.6% at 0.75 W/cm2. Therefore, DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nano-platform had broad application prospects in low power therapy and convenient imaging of carbon-based materials.

ce:section.ce:para: As a traditional therapy, the anti-tumor effect of chemotherapeutic drugs is limited [1–3], due to the multidrug resistance caused by P-glycoprotein efflux [4,5] and the systemic circulatory toxicity resulted from the lack of targeting [6–8]. The key to solving the dilemma of chemotherapeutic drugs is to achieve high effective and low toxic anti-tumor effect under precise treatment. Developmentally, combination therapy, which could markedly improve the therapeutic outcomes and minimize side effects, has become a vital strategy for efficaciously killing tumor cells. Many combination therapies have been designed, such as chemo-photodynamic therapy, chemo-photothermal therapy and chemo-immunotherapy and so on [9–12].

Fortunately, photothermal therapy (PTT), as a promising complementary treatment strategy, directly converts light radiation to sublethal or lethal cell hyperthermia for treating malignant tumor [13,14]. For PTT, due to the advantages of noninvasive manipulation and high tissue transparency, thus chemo-photothermal therapy has attracted extensive researchers [15,16]. Importantly, PTT partly ameliorates the tumor microenvironment, which facilitates the chemotherapeutic effect [17,18]. Mainly as displayed, high temperature generated by PTT could promote capillary dilation, improve blood perfusion and enable drug accumulation in tumor tissues. Furthermore, hyperthermia increases the fluidity and permeability of cell membrane, making it easy for cell to uptake drugs. Meanwhile, PTT induced cell to become sensitive to drugs and mitigated multidrug resistance. Multimodal synergistic tumor treatment, by combining chemotherapy with PTT, could harvest improved therapeutic efficacy and reduced side effects.

However, PTT strategy still suffers from inevitable damage to human normal cells and causes huge side effects to the human body under high power laser and duration time in further applications [19,20]. Therefore, it was extremely urgent to find photosensitive materials with higher photothermal conversion ability to achieve better photothermal effect and reduced toxicity under low-power irradiation. A large number of photothermal materials were explored, including metal materials (gold, cupric sulfide), inorganic carrier (black phosphorus, carbon) [21–23], etc. Benefiting from large specific surface area and good biocompatibility, hollow mesoporous carbon carriers have been used in photothermal therapy. Nevertheless, much application limitation was that the heating effect produced by individual carbon carrier required large irritation power. Thus, the combination of two photothermal agents brings a new idea for hyperthermia [24,25].

In recent years, polydopamine (PDA), with high adhesion, biocompatibility, biodegradability, as well as excellent light protection, has become a focus of increasing attention [26–28]. Featured with high photothermal conversion efficiency, PDA could be as an excellent photothermal agent, efficiently converting light into harmless heat energy [29–31]. Therefore, by modified on the surface of HMC, PDA cooperated with carbon carriers to have better photothermal conversion efficiency than individual material, achieving the goal of low power irradiation to ablate cancer cells and reduce toxicity to healthy issues. Additionally, the surface of PDA existed numerous functional groups, which could be connected covalently with other materials via Michael addition or Schiff base reaction, performing excellent modifiable feature [32,33]. PDA surface modification could promote the long circulation of nanoparticles and enhance the passive targeted aggregation through EPR effect. Importantly, as gating, PDA prevents the premature release of drugs, reducing the toxic effects of chemotherapy during circulation [34,35].

To date, fluorescence imaging techniques with significant advantages of simple operation, low cost, and good biocompatibility, have been combined with different therapy modalities for precise cancer treatment [36–39]. Differing other organic fluorescent probes of poor stability and water-dispersity, polyethyleneimine carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their excellent fluorescence imaging, high quantum yield and durative luminescence emission [40,41]. However, CDs emit blue fluorescence with limited tissues penetration, which greatly restricts their applications [42,43]. Meanwhile, carbon-based nanoparticles with forceful π-π conjugated structures led to fluorescence quenching of CDs, inducing the missing of imaging function. To surmount this dilemma, it is vital to extend the emission wavelength of CDs. A relatively feasible method is that CDs can be employed to develop composite materials based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect [44]. The main emission of CDs ranges from 400 nm to 600 nm, which has efficient overlap with the absorption of PDA. Meanwhile, the connection of CDs and PDA by covalent bonds ensured sufficient close distance. Thus, CDs with high energy blue emission can be used as an ideal energy donor and the PDA act as an acceptor. And obvious red shift can be observed (from blue to green) after the CDs were modified on the surface of PDA.

Therefore, a fluorescence imaging-guided low-power and high-efficiency enhanced thermo-chemotherapy anti-tumor nano-delivery platform was constructed in this paper. HMC with ultra-high specific surface area and photothermal conversion efficiency was selected as the vehicles to ensure the super-high loading of DOX. Subsequently, PDA with excellent biocompatibility was linked to HMC surface through disulfide bonds. Naturally, the superposition of the two photothermal materials plays a synergistic role in photothermal therapy, achieving better thermal ablation to kill malignant tumors and reduce tissue damage under lower power irradiation. PDA can also be used as a gating switch to endow the system with multiple responsive release characteristics of GSH/pH/NIR. In addition, based on the FRET principle, the system emits bright green light after grafting CDs on PDA, and the fluorescence spectrum shows that the emission wavelength has a significant red shift, which resolves the predicament of fluorescence quenching of carbon materials. Confocal laser microscopy (CLSM) showed that the system had excellent fluorescence imaging ability. Meanwhile, both in vitro and in vivo experiments have proved that the system has excellent anti-tumor effects, and has bright application prospects in imaging-guided enhanced thermo-chemotherapy synergistic therapy.

Dopamine hydrochloride, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 3-mercaptopropionic, doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), glutathione (GSH) and citric acid (CA) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (China). Polyethyleneimine (Branched PEI, MW = 25 kDa) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), RPMI-1640, 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) and Hoechst 33258 were supplied by Dalian Meilun Biotech. Com. Ltd. (Dalian, China). Other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2F30; FEI; Eindhoven, Nether-lands) was used to verify the morphological feature. The morphology of CDs was observed by the atomic force microscope (AFM, CypherES, Asylum Research, America).The physical state was evaluated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Geigerflex, Japan). Fourier transfer infrared (FT-IR) spectra were investigated on Bruker IFS (Switzerland). The pore size distribution and surface area were carried out on N2 adsorption/desorption analyzer (V-Sorb 2800P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, China). The particle sizes and Zeta potentials were evaluated by a Nanosizer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Cellular imaging was performed confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 510).

Functionalized CDs are easily synthesized through one step hydrothermal method according to our previous methods [45–47]. And HMC-COOH was prepared by the previous method [24,48]. The synthesis steps of HMC-SS-COOH are as follows. First, HMC-SS-Py was prepared by stirring HMC-COOH and Py-SS-NH2 (S-(2-Aminoethylthio)-2-thiopyridine hydrochloride). Subsequently, HMC-SS-Py and 3-mercaptopropionic acid were dispersed in ethanol and stirred for 24 h. Finally, HMC-SS-COOH was obtained by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and dried under vacuum.

40 mg of HMC-SS-COOH and 40 mg of DOX were mixed overnight in pH 7.4 PBS to collect the precipitation (named as DOX/HMC-SS-COOH) via centrifuging. Then, the resultant was re-suspended in 30 mL of Tris–HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.5) containing 20 mg of dopamine hydrochloride, followed by shaking at 37 °C for 6 h. After centrifugation, the obtained nanoparticles (named as DOX/HMC-SS-PDA) and 1 mg of CDs was stirred in Tris–HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.5) for 6 h, and then the final product was collected as DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs. The DOX loading content was calculated by subtracting the unloaded amounts of DOX in the supernatant during the whole operation processes and drug-loading efficiency (LE) was calculated by the equation. Meanwhile, HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles were prepared by the similar methods except for the usage of DOX.
LE
%
=
W a
−
W b
W c
In which Wawas the added mass of DOX, Wb was the mass of DOX in supernatant, and Wc was the final mass of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs.

To verify the enhanced photothermal capacity of PDA, the photothermal effects of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs samples were compared by irradiating various concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) with fixed 808 nm laser intensity of 0.75 W/cm2 and irradiating suspensions (200 μg/mL) at different power densities from 0.5 to 2 W/cm2. Additionally, the photothermal stability of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs were studied by repeatedly irradiating with 808 nm NIR laser to a specific temperature followed by cooling down to room temperature.

The photothermal conversion efficiency could be used as an index to evaluate the photothermal capability of nanoparticles. HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (100 μg/mL) were irradiated by an 808 nm NIR laser (1.0 W/cm2), respectively. The photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated [30]:
η =
hS
T max
−
T surr
−
Q Dis
I
1 −
10
− Aλ
where h is the heat-transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of the container, Tmax is the equilibrium temperature, Tsurr is room temperature, QDis is the heat dissipation due to the light absorbed by the quartz sample cell, I is the laser power, and Aλ is the absorbance at an excitation wavelength of 808 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The multi-stimuli triggered release of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The release behavior of DOX from presupposed pH/GSH/NIR responsive DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles was estimated in different PBS (pH 5.0 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS in the absence/presence of GSH) under a 37 °C shaking table. The sample suspensions were taken out at a predetermined time, followed by centrifugation and measurement of the released amount of DOX from the carriers at 480 nm. For NIR-triggered groups, the DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs suspensions were irradiated with an 808 nm laser (0.75 W/cm2) for 3 min before centrifugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The shedding mechanism of PDA and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the relatively low acidity and high concentration of GSH in tumor cells, the HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles were placed in PBS (pH 5.0, 6.5, 7.4) and GSH media (0, 3, 10 mM), respectively. After 12 h, the supernatant was centrifuged and collected, and the corresponding fluorescence signal was detected at 400 nm excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, the photothermal effect of supernatant was detected under 808 nm laser. Subsequently, the supernatant was further treated to remove PDA from the supernatant, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra and photothermal effects were detected.

In order to further explore the photothermal therapeutic effect of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs on cellular level in vitro, the 4T1 cells were cultivated in RPMI-1640 for 24 h. Followed by adding various concentrations of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (10, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL) and co-incubation for 2 h. Finally, the photothermal effects of the cell suspensions were detected at 808 nm laser irradiation.

Cellular uptake behavior of nano-compositions was investigated by utilizing CLSM and flow cytometry analysis (FCM). Briefly, 4T1 cells were seeded into 24-well plates and incubated with DOX and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (5 μg/mL of DOX) for 2 h, followed by staining the cell nuclei with Hoechst 33258 for CLSM imaging. Additionally, 4T1 cells were seeded onto 6-well plates for 24 h, followed by treated with DOX group and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (DOX concentration of 5 μg/mL) with or without 3 min laser irradiation. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of DOX was measured by FCM.

4T1 cells were seeded into 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. After co-incubation with HMC-SS-PDA@CDs, the cells were irradiated at 0.75 W/cm2 for different time. Subsequently, the cells were stained with Calcein-AM/PI and placed in CLSM for observation.

4T1 cells were seeded into 96-well agarose-containing plates at a density of 6 × 103 cells per well. After the diameter of 3D MCS reached about 300–400 μm, different treatment regimens were given. The changes of 3D MCS were observed by optical microscope and its diameter was recorded. After co-staining with Calcein-AM/PI, the 3D MCS were placed under CLSM to observe its survival.

Generally, the cytotoxicity of nano-platforms was evaluated by a MTT assay. 4T1 cells were incubated with RPMI-1640 in a 96-well plate (1 × 104 cells per well) for overnight, followed by treated with serial dilutions of DOX solution, HMC-SS-PDA@CDs, HMC-COOH, DOX/HMC-COOH and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (DOX concentrations varied from 0.01 to 10 μg/mL) for another 24 h. Subsequently, MTT solution was cultured for 4 h, followed by adding DMSO to dissolve formazan crystals. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm. Importantly, cells of photothermal treatment group were received an 808 nm laser at the density of 0.75 W/cm2 for 3 min. Meanwhile, combination index (CI) was calculated to estimate the synergistic effect of thermo-chemotherapy therapy based on previous reports [48,49].

18–20 g of female Balb/c mice were purchased from Shenyang Pharmaceutical University. The experimental procedures comply with the ARRIVE guidelines of Animal Protection and Ethics Committee of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University. Each mouse was subcutaneously injected with 1 × 107 4T1 cells. Then, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were used to carry out the biodistribution experiments. When the tumors were measured to reach 200 mm3 (volume = width2 × length/2), the animals were divided randomly into three groups. Each group was intravenously injected with DOX, DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR, at an equivalent DOX dose of 10 mg/kg. After 2, 6 or 12 h post injection, the mice were euthanized and the tumor and main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were harvested and analyzed by using IVIS imaging system (n = 3).

The in vivo synergistic antitumor activity of nanocomposite was investigated by 4T1 tumor-bearing Balb/c mice, which were randomly allocated into five groups (n = 5): (1) saline (control group); (2) DOX; (3) DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs; (4) HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR; (5) DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR. All mice were injected intravenously at 1 d, 4 d and 7 d with equivalent DOX dosage of 10 mg/kg for each mouse. Meanwhile, after 6 h of injection, NIR laser (808 nm, 0.75 W/cm2) for 3 min was introduced to HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR groups. Body weight and tumor volume were recorded every 2 d. Subsequently, all the mice were sacrificed after 15 d of treatment and the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were collected and used for hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining.

The synthetic process of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs is displayed in Scheme 1A. CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and the size and morphology of CDs were identified by AFM and TEM. As detected by TEM in Fig. 1D, CDs exhibited uniform dispersion and discrete quasi-spherical shape, with a well-defined structure of about 5 nm diameter. The AFM 3D image and topography image (Fig. 1A, B and C) further manifested spherical structure of CDs with the average height of 5 nm. Meanwhile, the XRD pattern (Fig. S1) revealed a broad peak at 22.5°, indicating the obvious graphitic structure of CDs [50,51].

The uniform-sized HMC-COOH was prepared according to our previously published methods. HMC-COOH was then functionalized with Py-SS-NH2 and 3-mercaptopropionic acid to synthesize HMC-SS-COOH. HMC-SS-PDA was obtained by an amidation reaction between amino groups of PDA and carboxyl groups of HMC-SS-COOH. Subsequently, the CDs-modified HMC-SS-PDA (HMC-SS-PDA@CDs) was acquired by Michael addition reaction or Schiff base reaction between PDA and CDs [52].

As revealed by Fig. 1E, HMC-SS-COOH showed the uniform and hierarchical hollow mesoporous structure with the average size and thickness of 130 nm and 30 nm, which laid a foundation for high drug loading capacity. After functional modifications, the particle size of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs increased slightly to 170 nm and hydration particle size was 212.2 nm measured by dynamic light scattering method (Figs. 1G and 2I). The visual observation of PDA layer demonstrated the successful graft, which also was verified by the blurry channel of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs compared to HMC-COOH. Additionally, the multifunctional modifications of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs could be characterized by various methods. As described in Fig. 2A, the surface area (SBET), pore size distribution (PD) and total pore volume (Vt) of HMC, HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs were measured by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and the relevant values were summarized in Table S1. Compared to bare HMC and HMC-COOH, the SBET (371.9 m2/g) and Vt (0.42 cm3/g) of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs were significantly diminished. In Fig. 2B, the Zeta potentials of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-COOH were −41.9 mV and − 39.7 mV due to the existence of abundant carboxyl groups. Moreover, after grafting PDA and CDs, the Zeta potentials was decreased to −31.3 mV and − 17.2 mV, which further confirmed the successful function of PDA and CDs. Meanwhile, FT-IR spectra showed that the stretching vibration of COOH at 1700 cm−1 disappeared after PDA coating (Fig. 2C). The surface of PDA is rich in a large number of amino groups, and the coating of PDA is through the binding of amino groups of PDA and carboxyl groups on the surface of HMC through amidation reaction. Therefore, after the coating of PDA, the carboxyl groups on the surface of HMC changed to amide bonds, so the carboxyl peak at 1700 cm−1 disappeared.

The successful modification of CDs could also be explored by fluorescence spectra. As seen in Fig. 2D and G, the fluorescence spectra of CDs showed a strong emission peak at 450 nm when excitation wavelength at 380 nm. After the CDs were decorated on the external of HMC-SS-PDA, the maximum emission wavelength of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was changed to 505 nm, showing the obvious red shift feature due to the FRET effect between PDA and CDs (in Fig. 2E and H). As an energy donor, CDs transferred the absorbed energy to PDA and realized the long shift of emission wavelength from blue light to green light, which improved the fluorescence imaging ability of carriers [52].

Notably, the stability of the nanocomposite was crucial for in vivo drug delivery. To explore the stability of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles in simulated physiological environment, the changes of particle size were monitored within 12 h. As can be seen in Fig. 2F, from 0 h to 12 h, HMC-SS-COOH flocculated and aggregated seriously in PBS (pH 7.4) and displayed the increased size, while HMC-SS-PDA@CDs retained well dispersity. And the corresponding photos are shown in Fig. S2. Thereby, the modification of PDA@CDs on the surface of HMC could obviously enhance the dispersity and stability of nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal properties of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To testify the enhanced photothermal generation efficiency of PDA, the comparison between HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs under 808 nm light was conducted. As seen in the Fig. 3A and D, the photothermal conversion characteristics of HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs were obvious concentration-dependent. With the equal concentration (200 μg/mL), the temperature of HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs were increased by 26.1 °C and 36.0 °C under 808 nm irradiation (0.75 W/cm2 for 5 min) and corresponding photographs were shown in Fig. 3I. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion ability of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs showed power-dependent property (Fig. 3B and E). Meanwhile, the vehicles still owned good photothermal stability after repeated heating as shown in Fig. 3C and F.

The reason of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs owning enhanced thermal ablation effect was explored by calculating photothermal conversion efficiency as shown in Fig. 3G and H. Compared with HMC-SS-COOH (η = 29.9%), the photothermal conversion efficiency of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was 35.9%. HMC-SS-PDA@CDs showed the higher photothermal conversion efficiency mainly due to the synergistic heating effect of the two photothermal agents HMC and PDA, which made HMC-SS-PDA@CDs have a higher photothermal performance under the same conditions. In conclusion, high photothermal conversion efficiency of PDA@CDs modified HMC-SS-COOH played synergic heating effect, which provided feasibility for promoting low-power clinical treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shedding mechanism of PDA and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles were dispersed in gradient concentration pH and GSH to explore the shedding mechanism of PDA and CDs. Followed by shaking for 12 h, the supernatant were collected separately to implement fluorescence detection. Interestingly, bright green fluorescence rather than blue fluorescence could be observed in the supernatant, and the fluorescence intensity reached its maximum at high concentration of GSH and low pH conditions (Fig. 4A and B). We speculate that the source of green fluorescence was PDA@CDs complex rather than pure CDs. To verify this conjecture, the oxidation method was employed to remove PDA from the supernatant. Excitedly, the blue fluorescence of CDs could be observed in the treatment solution as shown in Fig. 4C. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra demonstrated that the emission wavelength of the solution shifted from 505 nm to 460 nm, which further indicated that the treated solution was mainly CDs. Therefore, the shedding mechanism of PDA@CDs was deduced from cooperative effects of GSH and pH value as displayed in Fig. 4D. The disulfide bond connecting the PDA@CDs to HMC nanoparticle could facilely be reduced by GSH, causing the natural shedding of PDA@CDs from HMC. Meanwhile, several literatures have reported the degradation behavior of PDA in acidic environment [53,54]. Thus, the partial degradation of PDA would induce the shedding of PDA@CDs from HMC surface.

Meanwhile, the photothermal effect of the supernatant was also detected. The temperature of supernatant increased with the increase of GSH content. In the presence of 10 mM GSH, the temperature of the supernatant increased from 25.9 °C to 37.8 °C (Fig. 4F). And the pH-dependent warming appeared in the supernatants treated with different pH conditions as shown in Fig. 4E. However, the supernatant hardly warmed up after the removal of PDA in Fig. 4G. The above results further indicated that PDA and CDs shed in the form of PDA@CDs complex, and high concentration of GSH and low pH were the inducing factors for their shedding.

To explore the potential of tailoring HMC-SS-PDA@CDs as a controlled drug delivery carrier, DOX as a guest model drug was encapsulated in the channel of nanocarrier. And the loading capacity of DOX in HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was up to 41%. The ultrahigh loading efficiency was achieved by the enormous specific surface area of mesoporous hollow structure, powerful adsorption ability, as well as the considerable noncovalent interactions among molecules such as π-π stacking and electrostatic force [24,48]. Furthermore, the physical state of DOX was characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 5A, when DOX was loaded into the vehicles, the characteristic crystal diffraction peak disappeared obviously, which elucidated that DOX was enclosed in rigid channel of HMC materials in an amorphous state.

Subsequently, to determine whether the DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs composition could meet the requirements for effective control of drug release, the release behavior of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was evaluated at gradient pH values. The accumulative DOX release increased from 9.8% in pH 7.4 PBS to 18.0% in pH 5.0 PBS (in Fig. 5B), showing the pH-dependent DOX release behavior. This phenomenon could be due to the partial detaching and hydrolysis of the PDA layer from the HMC-SS-PDA@CDS surface under acidic conditions [6]. Also, acid condition weakened electrostatic force between DOX and vehicles, promoting drug DOX escaping from the channels of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs [55,56].

Furthermore, the GSH-stimulated release feature was also assessed. As shown in Fig. 5B, the cumulative release amount of DOX reached 33.2% (pH 5.0 PBS, 10 mM GSH), which was much higher than that of in pH 5.0 PBS (18.0%). The same phenomenon occurred in pH 7.4 PBS with or without 10 mM GSH. These results well elucidated that the disulfide bonds between PDA and HMC particles were cracked under the condition of high concentration of GSH, thus unlocking the drug release from the mesoporous channels.

Additionally, the release behavior of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs at different pH was observed under 808 nm laser illuminations. Remarkably, as shown in Fig. 5C and D, a significant thermo-triggered burst release of DOX from vehicles was found at 1 h, 4 h and 9 h when NIR-laser was executed. The NIR-responsive release patterns could be due to that the combined-photothermal effect of HMC-COOH and PDA@CDs accelerated the drug molecular motion and made DOX escape easily from the carriers [57,58].

In conclusion, such pH/NIR/GSH triple-responsive DOX release patterns endowed DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs system great potential for controlled drug release in the specificity of tumor microenvironment (TME) and laid the foundation for the synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal therapeutic effect on cytological level
ce:section.ce:para: In previous studies, it has been proved that HMC-SS-PDA@CDs suspension has strong photothermal conversion ability. To further examine the photothermal capabilities of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles, cellular photothermal experiment was conducted. As shown in Fig. S3, the cellular photothermal effect increased significantly as the increased concentration of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs. Appreciably, when 1 W/cm2 laser density was applied to cellular suspensions containing HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles at a concentration of 100 μg/mL, the temperature rapidly raised to 49.8 °C within 180 s. Generally, tumor cells will be killed under the temperature above 42 °C [48]. Meanwhile, the results of AM/PI staining experiment proved that the cells could be completely killed under the hyperthermic condition as shown in Fig. 6A. With the extension of irradiation time, the cells were gradually stained red (PI, dead cells) and the green fluorescence (AM, living cells) gradually disappeared. Evidently, when the irradiation time was extended to 7 min, strong red fluorescence could be observed and the green fluorescence basically disappeared. Similarly, trypan blue staining assay was used to identify dead and living cells. As shown in Fig. S4, with the increase of irradiation power densities, the cells were gradually stained blue, and the cell morphology changed significantly at the high power density. These results indicated that the HMC-SS-PDA@CDs could be as a unique agent for tumor treatment, attributed to its excellent photothermal effect and outstanding ability to effectively kill tumor cells.

Efficient cellular uptake was a prerequisite for the delivery of anticancer drugs, thus, CLSM was used to reveal the cellular uptake efficiency of the preparation. As shown in Fig. 6B, the bright red fluorescence from DOX was mainly localized in the nucleus. Meanwhile, there was no significant difference in red fluorescence intensity before and after NIR irradiation. Naturally, due to the FRET effect between PDA and CDs, bright green fluorescence could be observed after the vehicles were ingested. However, only very weak DOX fluorescence was observed in the perinuclear region in DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs group, indicating that the nanoparticles were effectively internalized and slowly released DOX. After NIR treatment, the increased fluorescence intensity of DOX and PDA@CDs was observed. The reason for this result may be that hyperthermia made the improvement of the permeability and fluidity of the cell membrane, easily facilitating the vehicles endocytosed into the cell. Furthermore, the hyperthermia increased the thermal motion of DOX molecules and weakened the force between the carrier and drugs, contributing to the fast release of drugs. Meanwhile, FCM quantitatively evaluated the cellular uptake behavior of free DOX and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs by detecting intracellular DOX fluorescence (Fig. 6C), which was accordance with the CLSM results.

In vitro MTT assay was conducted to evaluate tumor killing activity. After incubation with HMC-SS-PDA@CDs and HMC-COOH nanoparticles without laser irradiation, the 4T1 cells viability over 80.0%, exhibiting the excellent biocompatibility and safety of vehicles (Fig. 6E). However, the cell viability decreased markedly with the incremental concentration of HMC-SS-PDA@CDs with (Fig. 6D) or without NIR (Fig. 6E) laser irradiation, illustrating that HMC-SS-PDA@CDs showed admirable photothermal conversion feature and could convert the NIR laser energy into cytotoxic hyperpyrexia. Expectedly, the cell viability of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs with NIR irradiation was lower than that of free DOX group and HMC-SS-PDA@CDs group at the same drug concentration, displaying the better therapeutic efficiency than chemotherapy or photothermal therapy alone. The effective cytotoxicity of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs can be attributed to the cellular hyperthermia caused by PTT and the accelerated release of DOX at elevated temperature, achieving a significant synergistic effect of photothermal ablation and chemotherapy. Also, the IC50 value of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR was the lowest among all groups as shown in Table 1 . The combination index (CI) of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs (0.348) was lower than that of DOX/HMC-COOH (0.613) under the same NIR irradiation. In conclusion, these results fully illustrated that PDA@CDs-modified DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles exhibited outstanding inhibition on tumor cells by enhanced photothermal effect combined with chemotherapy.

To overcome the limitations of 2D cell model, 3D MCS were used as a model to verify the therapeutic effect. As shown in Fig. 7A, all treatment groups had different degrees of collapse and dissociation. Especially, for the combined treatment group, 3D MCS almost disappeared after 5 d treatment. The same results were observed in the AM/PI fluorescence of 3D MCS (Fig. 7B). The combination treatment group emitted intense red fluorescence (dead cells), and the green fluorescence (living cells) basically disappeared. Meanwhile, the results of fluorescence quantitative analysis showed that the DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR group had 97.1% killing effect on 3D MCS (Fig. 7C). The above exciting findings laid a firm foundation for subsequent experiments in vivo.

To investigate the accumulation capacity of nanoparticles in different organs and tumor tissues, the in vivo biodistribution of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs was explored in female BALB/c mice bearing 4T1 tumor. As displayed in Fig. 8A, for DOX solution group, the fluorescence intensity at tumor sites decreased significantly with the extension of time. When drug was loaded into the carrier, the DOX fluorescence signal of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs group reached the maximum at 6 h due to the long systemic circulation effect of PDA. Moreover, the accumulation of DOX in the tumor was decreased at 12 h due to the slow clearance of DOX. By contrast, for the group of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs with NIR laser irradiation, the fluorescence signal of DOX in tumors was gradually enhanced over time from 2 to 12 h, indicating that the increased temperature of tumor facilitated drug release and cell internalization. These results were well in line with the profiles of drug release in vitro. The quantification results as shown in Fig. 8B exhibited significant distinction between DOX and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR. Compared to the free DOX group, the DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR group exhibited 3.25-fold higher fluorescence intensity in tumor tissues at 12 h, which indicated that the modification of PDA@CDs further improved the in vivo long-term circulation of nanocomposites.

Encouraged by the favorable in vitro enhanced photothermal and combined chemo therapy and PTT of DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs, the in vivo combined antitumor was further examined using 4T1 tumor model. Fig. S5 showed the thermal images of the tumor region of mice treated with DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs with 808 nm laser irradiation. The average temperature of the tumor region rapidly increased to 52.4 °C after treatment with 808 nm laser at 0.75 W/cm2 within 180 s, which is high enough for photothermal ablation of tumors in vivo. Meanwhile, the growth rates of tumors were slowed in the monotherapy groups mice (Free DOX, HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR and DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs), while the combined treatment group with DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs + NIR group significantly reduced the growth of tumors and the tumor inhibition rate reached 92.6% (Fig. 8C, D and F). Although DOX solution had certain inhibitory effect on tumor growth, body weight was significantly decreased (Fig. 8E), displaying that free DOX had significant toxicity to normal tissues. As expected, the toxicity of DOX could be reduced after DOX was loaded into the functional carriers and no significant differences in body weight were observed between the treatment and saline groups. All above results suggested that the DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs nanoparticles exhibited excellent photothermal-enhanced thermo-chemotherapy synergistic effect and biocompatibility.

Subsequently, the sections of major organs and tumor from saline and treatment groups were collected for pathology analysis by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining (Fig. 8G). DOX group had certain cardiotoxicity, while no obvious inflammation or lesions were observed in each organ of the treatment groups. These results suggested that DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs performed excellent biocompatibility in vivo.

In summary, a novel fluorescence imaging-guided HMC-based delivery platform (HMC-SS-PDA@CDs) was designed for low-power enhanced thermo-chemotherapy. The ultra-high specific surface area and hollow structure of HMC ensured the high loading capacity of 41%. The pH-sensitive PDA and CDs were connected to the surface of HMC through reduction-sensitive disulfide bonds, which endowed the system with pH/NIR/GSH multi-responsive release characteristics. And the excellent photothermal performance (η = 35.9%) induced by the combined PDA and HMC laid the foundation for low-power synergistic thermal chemotherapy. Additionally, the PDA@CDs made the fluorescence emission wavelength red-shifted and showed bright green fluorescence, which solved the fluorescence quenching dilemma of porous carbon. Cellular uptake assay proved the fluorescence imaging ability of nanoparticles and the accelerated drug release under the NIR irradiation. Furthermore, DOX/HMC-SS-PDA@CDs showed excellent synergistic thermo-chemotherapy with CI of 0.348 at cytological level as well as at 3D tumor spheres and in vivo anti-tumor experiments at a low power irradiation of 0.75 W/cm2. This present drug delivery platform would have broad application prospects in low-power therapy and convenient imaging of carbon-based materials.
Junya Lu: Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing;
Kaili Wang: Resources, Formal analysis;
Wei Lei: Validation, Investigation;
Yuling Mao: Review & Editing;
Donghua Di: Review & Editing;
Qinfu Zhao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Review, Project administration;
Siling Wang: Supervision, Review, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81603058), Liaoning Provincal National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 2019-ZD-0460) and Youth Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (No. 2017LQN20) are greatly acknowledged.
